result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sold me the strangest tomato I'd ever seen. Purple-black, almost iridescent, heavy as a paperweight. "From heritage seeds," she said, winking. "My grandmother's grandmother's recipe for happiness." I laughed, paid three dollars, went home.

That night I sliced it open. The inside gleamed like a geode, crystalline and impossible. It tasted of summer storms and copper pennies, of first kisses and last goodbyes. I dreamed in colors that don't exist.

Morning came different. The coffee maker spoke in Morris code. The cat walked backwards. My reflection arrived late to the mirror, apologizing. Outside, the trees grew downward into the sky.

I called in sick to work. What else could I do? My voice came out as saxophone music. My boss, somehow, understood perfectly.

The tomato sat on my counter, regenerating. Each slice I'd cut had grown back, but wrong—one piece now showed a tiny television playing 1950s commercials, another contained a miniature thunderstorm, complete with lightning the size of eyelashes. I ate another piece. This one tasted of my childhood dog's fur and my mother's disappointment.

By noon, gravity had become optional. I chose to remain grounded, mostly, though my hair disagreed. The mailman delivered tomorrow's mail. I paid yesterday's bills. Time, it seemed, had given up on linear thinking.

The woman from the farmer's market knocked on my door at sunset, or sunrise—hard to tell when the sun was bouncing. "How's the tomato?" she asked. Her eyes were kaleidoscopes.

"Peculiar," I admitted.

"Good," she said. "Peculiar is underrated. People want normal tomatoes, normal lives, normal deaths. But you—" she pointed at my chest, where my heart was glowing faintly through my shirt "—you paid three dollars for peculiar. That means something."

"What does it mean?"

"Haven't figured that out yet. Been selling these tomatoes for three hundred years, give or take a Tuesday. Everyone reacts different. Last fellow turned into a library. Nice man, terrible overdue fees."

She left me with instructions: water it with moonlight, sing it the news, never let it touch mathematics. "It'll sort itself out," she promised. "Or you will. Or both. Or neither. Honestly, I've lost track."

Week two: the tomato had grown into a plant that produced fruit in the shape of questions. Why? When? Who? I ate them all, gained no answers, lost several assumptions. My apartment developed rooms that hadn't been there before—a conservatory full of extinct plants, a bathroom where the water ran upward, a closet that opened onto different seasons depending on my mood.

My sister visited. She didn't notice anything unusual, just complained about my coffee (which was now sentient and slightly offended). We talked about her divorce, my job, our father's health. Normal things, while my furniture rearranged itself politely around our conversation. The tomato plant leaned in, listening.

"You seem different," she finally said.

"How?"

"More... yourself. But also less. If that makes sense."

It did. Perfect sense. I was becoming undiluted, concentrated, like orange juice in reverse. Each day I shed another expectation, another supposed-to-be. The tomato plant approved, growing new fruits shaped like exclamation points.

Month two: reporters arrived. Someone had noticed my apartment building occasionally flickering between dimensions. They wanted explanations. I offered them tomatoes. Most declined. One brave soul accepted, bit into it, immediately became fluent in the language of clouds. She thanked me in cumulus, departed as a light drizzle.

The authorities tried to intervene. Building inspectors, health department, a theoretical physicist, two priests, and someone claiming to be from a government agency that didn't exist yet. The tomato plant grew a fruit shaped like a bureaucracy—endless, recursive, hollow at the center. I fed it to their paperwork. The paperwork resigned.

My mother called. "I had the strangest dream about you," she said. "You were growing backwards into yourself, becoming the child you never were."

"That's not a dream," I told her. "That's Tuesday."

She laughed, the sound turning into butterflies on my end of the line. They circled the tomato plant, which had now taken over my living room, producing fruits in shapes I couldn't name—some geometric impossibilities, others emotional states made edible.

The old woman returned on a Thursday that felt like September.

"Time to harvest," she announced.

"Harvest what?"

"Whatever you've grown."

I looked at my apartment, my life, myself. Everything was exactly wrong in exactly the right way. The tomato plant had taught me the secret: normalcy is a mass hallucination, reality is negotiable, and happiness comes in purple-black, iridescent packages that cost three dollars at farmer's markets.

We harvested together—not the fruits, but the spaces between them. The pauses where possibility lived. The moment before choosing, when all paths remain open. We bottled it in mason jars, labeled them "Maybe."

"What now?" I asked.

"Now?" She smiled, her kaleidoscope eyes spinning. "Now you plant the next one. Or become one. Or refuse the whole thing and demand your three dollars back. I never give refunds, but I admire the asking."

She left me with seeds that hummed lullabies and a business card that changed its text depending on who was reading it. For me, it said: "Congratulations on your peculiar revolution."

The tomato plant waved goodbye, grew one last fruit shaped like a beginning, then planted itself elsewhere, leaving only the scent of storm clouds and possibility.

I went back to work. My saxophone voice had mellowed to jazz piano. My reflection had learned punctuality. The cat walked forward again, but skeptically.

Everything was different. Everything was the same.

I kept one seed, just in case. It sits in my pocket, warm as a promise, patient as tomorrow, strange as a three-dollar tomato from a woman who's been selling them for three hundred years.

Some days, I almost plant it.

Most days, the almost is enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
